region_of_origin: Singapore (Indian community; dish originates from Karnataka, South India)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani class; no specific Donne Biryani or mutton Donne Biryani subclass exists. Mapped to closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Donne Biryani code. A0FKP (mixed meat and rice dish) is the closest defensible anchor. Mutton-specific biryani subtype not enumerated in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept for Donne Biryani or mutton biryani found in the SNOMED CT International Edition as of 2024.

preparation_method: served in a donne (areca leaf bowl)
main_ingredients: whole spices (cinnamon, clove, cardamom, bay leaf)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard donne (areca leaf bowl) serving of Mutton Donne Biryani typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising rice, mutton pieces, and accompanying gravy as served at Indian restaurants and hawker stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Donne Biriyani — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Donne Biriyani (cr_id G00010755). All per-100g macros, micronutrients, and minerals are taken verbatim from this record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central lamb/mutton cooked data (USDA SR Legacy #17290) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed rice and meat dish (biryani-type)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Donne Biryani. GI estimated at 58 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked basmati rice (GI ~58) and mixed meat-rice dishes of similar composition. The carbohydrate content of this dish is 7.44 g/100g; at a 350 g serving, available carbohydrate = ~26 g. GL = (58 × 26) / 100 = 15.1 (medium GL). Estimation basis: rice is the primary carbohydrate source; mutton and fat content slow gastric emptying, consistent with a medium GI for the composite dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467 mg/100g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,635 mg sodium, exceeding 70% of the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request reduced salt during preparation, avoid additional condiments or pickles, and ensure the remainder of the day's meals are very low in sodium to stay within the 2,000 mg daily target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 7.44 g/100g with sugar 4.69 g/100g; high protein (14.05 g/100g) moderates glycaemic response, but total carbohydrate load at a full 350 g serving reaches ~26 g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume a controlled portion (150–200 g) of Mutton Donne Biryani; the high protein content slows gastric emptying and moderates postprandial glucose rise, but total meal carbohydrate — including any accompanying rice, bread, or sugary condiments — must be counted and kept within the individual's carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 11.58 g/100g with estimated saturated fat ~4.8 g/100g; cholesterol 85 mg/100g; sodium 467 mg/100g; mutton is a red meat associated with elevated LDL-C and cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Mutton Donne Biryani to occasional consumption (no more than once per week), keep portions to 150 g or less, and pair with high-fibre vegetables to partially offset the saturated fat and sodium burden; the combination of red meat, saturated fat, cholesterol, and high sodium makes this dish a high-concern food for CVD management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory cytokine pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6); estimated saturated fat ~4.8 g/100g further contributes to pro-inflammatory load.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit or avoid Mutton Donne Biryani due to its red meat content and pro-inflammatory fatty acid profile; anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, cinnamon, clove) present in the dish provide partial offset but do not neutralise the inflammatory burden of regular red meat consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Donne Biryani is a South Indian-origin rice dish popular in Singapore's Indian community, traditionally served in a donne — a bowl fashioned from dried areca (betel nut) leaves that imparts a subtle earthy aroma to the food. The dish consists of bone-in mutton pieces slow-cooked with whole spices (cinnamon, clove, cardamom, bay leaf), ginger, garlic, tomato, green chilli, yogurt, and ghee, then layered and cooked together with short-grain or basmati rice using the dum method, which seals steam inside the vessel to allow the rice to absorb the spiced meat juices. The result is a richly flavoured, moist biryani with tender mutton, aromatic rice, and a complex spice profile that is simultaneously warming, savoury, and mildly pungent. In Singapore, it is commonly sold at Indian Muslim hawker stalls and restaurants, often only on Fridays — a tradition linked to the Muslim Jumu'ah (Friday prayer) meal culture. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 192 kcal, 14.05 g protein, 11.58 g fat, and 7.44 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish relative to typical biryani. Sodium is elevated at 467 mg/100g, making it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The high protein content moderates glycaemic response, making it conditionally suitable for Type 2 Diabetes in controlled portions. Mutton's red meat classification and arachidonic acid content make it pro-inflammatory and unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.
common_user_queries: Is Donne Biryani good for diabetics?
Sodium content of Mutton Donne Biryani
Can I eat Mutton Donne Biryani if I have high blood pressure?
What is a donne in Donne Biryani?
Why is Donne Biryani only on Fridays?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and micronutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010755, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy lamb/mutton cooked data as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; this single estimated field does not reduce overall confidence to medium given the strong primary source for all other values.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.8 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #17290 (Lamb, domestic, composite of trimmed retail cuts, cooked) as HPB Foodo does not report saturated fat breakdown for this dish. This is the only field not directly sourced from HPB. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Singapore hawker preparation of Mutton Donne Biryani are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked basmati rice; no direct GI measurement for Mutton Donne Biryani exists in any approved GI source. 4. The dish name includes '(Only Fridays)' in the original HPB record, reflecting a hawker-stall availability pattern; the canonical food name omits this scheduling qualifier. 5. Halal status is assessed as likely true given the Indian Muslim hawker context and mutton (not pork) base, but individual vendor certification should be verified.
